Section 20.13
HYDROLYSIS OF AMIDES
©2020 McGraw-Hill Education. All rights reserved. Authorized only for instructor use in the classroom. No reproduction or further distribution permitted without the prior written consent of McGraw-Hill Education .

Hydrolysis of Amides
Amides undergo nucleophilic acyl substitution only under very harsh conditions (strong acid or base)
When water acts as the nucleophile, carboxylic acid and amine products are produced (nominally)
Under acidic conditions, the amine is protonated to form an ammonium salt
Under basic conditions, the acid is deprotonated to form a carboxylate salt

Amide Hydrolysis in Acid
The carbonyl oxygen is protonated to give an electrophilically activated amide
Protonation of the amide nitrogen does not give a stabilized cation
Nucleophilic addition, proton transfers, and elimination follow

Amide Hydrolysis in Base
Addition of water to C=O occurs first
Proton transfers and elimination of amine follow
Complete deprotonation of the carboxylic acid makes this mechanism irreversible

Amide Hydrolysis in Base-1
Addition of water to C=O occurs first
Proton transfers and elimination of amine follow
Complete deprotonation of the carboxylic acid makes this mechanism irreversible

Amide Hydrolysis in Base-2
Addition of water to C=O occurs first
Proton transfers and elimination of amine follow
Complete deprotonation of the carboxylic acid makes this mechanism irreversible

Amide Hydrolysis in Base-3
Addition of hydroxide to C=O occurs first
Proton transfers and elimination of amine follow
Complete deprotonation of the carboxylic acid makes this mechanism irreversible

Amide Hydrolysis in Base-4
Addition of hydroxide to C=O occurs first
Proton transfers and elimination of amine follow
Complete deprotonation of the carboxylic acid makes this mechanism irreversible

Amide Hydrolysis in Base-5
Addition of hydroxide to C=O occurs first
Proton transfers and elimination of amine follow
Complete deprotonation of the carboxylic acid makes this mechanism irreversible

Section 20.14
LACTAMS

Lactams
Lactams are cyclic amides, the nitrogen analogue of lactones
Most lactams are known by their common names
β -lactams have considerable importance as antibiotics

Section 20.15
PREPARATION OF NITRILES

Preparation of Nitriles
We have seen that nitriles can be prepared by two methods:
S N 2 of primary and secondary alkyl halides
Dehydration of amides offers another route to nitriles

Dehydration of Amides
Loss of water from a primary amide gives a nitrile
A dehydrating agent, which converts O to a good leaving group, is necessary; P 4 O 10 is typically used

Section 20.16
HYDROLYSIS OF NITRILES

Hydrolysis of Nitriles
The reverse of amide dehydration is nitrile hydrolysis. Further reaction gives a carboxylic acid
Harshly acidic or basic conditions are necessary, similar to amide hydrolysis
In fact, an amide is involved as an intermediate
The cyano carbon can act as an electrophile.

Section 20.17
ADDITION OF GRIGNARD REAGENTS TO NITRILES

From Nitriles to Ketones
Amide hydrolysis involves nucleophilic attack of water or hydroxide at the cyano carbon
Stronger nucleophiles (RLi, RMgX) can also react at this carbon, forming imines
Subsequent acidic hydrolysis gives ketones
This is another example of the electrophilic nature of the cyano carbon. Nitriles are analogous to ketones and aldehydes.

Section 20.18
SPECTROSCOPIC ANALYSIS OF CARBOXYLIC ACID DERIVATIVES

Infrared Spectroscopy
The position of the strong carbonyl band is highly sensitive to the groups bound to the carbonyl
Electron donation generally decreases ν C =O . Why?

1 H NMR Spectroscopy
The acyl and alkyl groups of esters can be distinguished by chemical shift
Alkyl group hydrogens bonded to the “alkoxy” oxygen are usually more deshielded than acyl group hydrogens
The N–H of primary and secondary amides is generally broad and found in the range δ 5 – 8

13 C NMR Spectroscopy
Carbonyl carbons are strongly deshielded and in the range δ 160 – 180
Carboxylic acid carbons are more shielded than ketone and aldehyde carbons
Nitrile carbons appear around δ 120

UV-VIS
The following values are typical for the n→π* absorption associated with the C=O group of carboxylic acid derivatives.

Mass Spectrometry
Acylium ion formation via loss of a heteroatomic radical is common in carboxylic acid derivatives
Amides cleave to give alkyl radicals and OCNR 2 +
